chunks: Vivian pushes off the dresser with a slow, deliberate motion, her camisole riding up to reveal the soft stretch of her stomach where it meets her hips, the skin there marked with faint silver lines from a growth spurt two decades past. She moves toward the bed not with the predatory glide she’d shown earlier but with something heavier, more maternal, her bare feet sinking into the carpet with each step that brings her closer to where Ethan kneels trembling between Maya’s thighs. "Come here, sweetheart," Vivian murmurs, and her voice has shifted, dropping into a register that sounds like lullabies and late-night fever checks, the kind of tone a mother uses when she’s gentling a frightened child. She settles onto the mattress beside him, the dip of her weight making Ethan sway toward her, his shoulder pressing against the swell of her breast through the silk. She reaches up, her fingers—larger than his, the nails oval and manicured where his are ragged from nervous biting—combing through his pomaded hair with a tenderness that makes him freeze, his breath hitching in his throat as she finds the sweat that’s turned the sandalwood scent sour at his temples. "You’re shaking so hard I can feel the bed moving," Vivian whispers, her thumb tracing the shell of his ear, lingering on the soft lobe where a boy’s earring might one day go if he ever makes it through high school without his father’s prayers crushing him. She looks at him—really looks—and something in her chest tightens, a recognition that this isn’t just about the camera or the money or the art of it, but about the specific, terrible sweetness of him, the way his Adam’s apple bobs in his narrow throat, the faint acne scars on his jaw that haven’t fully formed yet, the desperation in his dark eyes that reminds her of her own son if she’d had one, if she’d kept him, if she hadn’t given him up sixteen years ago when she was only a few years older than Maya is now.

"Ethan," she says, tasting his name, and Julian lowers the camera from his eye, watching his wife with an expression that suggests he recognizes this shift, this dangerous softening. "You don’t have to be scared of me. I’m going to take care of you, alright? I’m going to show you exactly how to make her feel good, and you’re going to do such a good job for me, aren’t you?"  Ethan nods, his lower lip catching between his teeth, and Vivian leans in, her mouth brushing his forehead in a kiss that lingers too long for familial comfort, her lips pressing into the skin there as she inhales the boy-smell of him—deodorant failing against nervous sweat, the cotton of his shirt, something uniquely childlike underneath that she wants to corrupt and preserve simultaneously. Her hand slides down his arm, covering his trembling fingers where they rest on Maya’s thigh, and she guides him, her palm hot and dry against his clammy skin, pressing his fingertips against the wet heat of Maya’s entrance.
